competency_level_descriptors.extending: I apply multiple criteria, weigh sources differently across them, and state which criterion most shifted my overall view.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can identify at least two algorithmic, platform, or production-incentive factors that shaped how a contested claim reached me.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band E.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I describe how I came across the claim ('I saw it on X') without identifying any algorithmic or platform factor.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I identify one factor (for example, an algorithm prioritises engagement) but do not link it to why this specific claim reached me.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can identify at least two algorithmic, platform, or production-incentive factors that shaped how a contested claim reached me.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I identify multiple factors and trace how each specifically shaped whether I saw this claim, which framing I saw, and what adjacent content surrounded it.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can name the self-bias most likely to affect my interpretation of a contested claim (confirmation, motivated reasoning, in-group alignment).

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band E.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I list biases in the abstract without identifying which one is most likely to affect me on this specific claim.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name a bias but pick a less-uncomfortable one rather than the bias most clearly at work in my reaction.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can name the self-bias most likely to affect my interpretation of a contested claim (confirmation, motivated reasoning, in-group alignment).
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I name the most-likely self-bias and point to specific moves in my own reasoning where the bias was visible.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can justify my conclusion about a contested claim with explicit awareness of the self-bias I named.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band E.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I state a conclusion without referring back to the bias I named.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I refer to the bias in the conclusion but as an afterthought rather than as a constraint on how I reasoned.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can justify my conclusion about a contested claim with explicit awareness of the self-bias I named.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I justify my conclusion and show how I adjusted my reasoning to account for the named bias, including where I held a view more tentatively than my gut wanted.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can apply detection and verification methods appropriate to a real-world contested claim involving synthetic, AI-generated, or sophisticated disinformation.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band F.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I apply methods built for ordinary misinformation that do not engage the synthetic or AI-generated character of the claim.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I apply detection methods appropriate to synthetic content but use only one method and miss cross-verification.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can apply detection and verification methods appropriate to a real-world contested claim involving synthetic, AI-generated, or sophisticated disinformation.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I apply multiple methods appropriate to the kind of disinformation in play and document what each method revealed or failed to reveal.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can compare my verification process against a recognised critical-appraisal framework (e.g. SIFT, Wineburg's professional fact-checker heuristics).

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band F.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I describe my process without comparing it to a recognised framework.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I reference a framework but my comparison stays at the level of overall approval rather than examining specific steps.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can compare my verification process against a recognised critical-appraisal framework (e.g. SIFT, Wineburg's professional fact-checker heuristics).
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I compare my process against the named framework step by step, identify where I followed it, where I departed, and what I would do differently next time.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can construct a reasoned conclusion that states what I believe, what I would share, what I would act on, and specifically what I cannot know and why.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band F.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I state a single overall conclusion without separating believe, share, act, or acknowledging what I cannot know.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I separate some of the four elements (believe, share, act, what-I-cannot-know) but the 'what I cannot know' line is generic.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can construct a reasoned conclusion that states what I believe, what I would share, what I would act on, and specifically what I cannot know and why.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I separate all four elements and explain specifically why the unknowable parts are unknowable (technical limits, source inaccessibility, genuine contestation).

criteria.criterion_statement: I can identify one specific persuasive-design feature in a chosen digital product using correct vocabulary.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band C.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I describe what the product does but do not use any persuasive-design vocabulary.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I attempt persuasive-design vocabulary but apply terms loosely or to the wrong feature.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can identify one specific persuasive-design feature in a chosen digital product using correct vocabulary.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I identify a specific persuasive-design feature using correct vocabulary and distinguish it from features that look similar but are not the same mechanism.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe one reason a persuasive-design feature works on people, drawing on dopamine, reward, or habit-loop mechanism.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band C.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I say the feature works because 'people like it' without referencing a specific mechanism.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I reference a mechanism (dopamine, reward, habit loop) but my description of how it works is inaccurate or generic.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can describe one reason a persuasive-design feature works on people, drawing on dopamine, reward, or habit-loop mechanism.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I describe how the mechanism works on people with enough specificity to predict when the feature would be more or less effective.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can explain one specific effect a chosen digital product has had on me in time, mood, or behaviour.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band C.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I say the product affects me 'a lot' or 'a little' without naming a specific effect.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name an effect but without enough specificity to tell whether the product caused it or only correlated with it.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can explain one specific effect a chosen digital product has had on me in time, mood, or behaviour.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I explain a specific effect with concrete examples and note where I can tell the product caused the effect versus where I am inferring.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can identify at least two interacting persuasive-design features in a chosen digital product using correct vocabulary.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I identify one persuasive-design feature in isolation, without recognising interaction.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I identify two features but treat them as independent additions rather than as interacting mechanisms.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can identify at least two interacting persuasive-design features in a chosen digital product using correct vocabulary.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I identify multiple interacting features and show how one feature amplifies or depends on another (for example, notifications amplifying variable reward).

criteria.criterion_statement: I can analyse how each persuasive-design feature shapes attention, emotion, or behaviour in the product.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I describe features without analysing which element of attention, emotion, or behaviour each one shapes.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I analyse one element (usually attention) but miss how the features also shape emotion or behaviour.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can analyse how each persuasive-design feature shapes attention, emotion, or behaviour in the product.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I analyse how each feature shapes specific elements of the attention/emotion/behaviour system and identify which effect is primary and which is secondary.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can explain one specific effect a chosen digital product has had on my own thinking, mood, or time.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I state a general effect on me without naming whether it falls on thinking, mood, or time.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name a specific effect but I do not ground it in examples from my actual use.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can explain one specific effect a chosen digital product has had on my own thinking, mood, or time.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I explain a specific effect with grounded examples and reflect on where the effect has spilled into domains outside the product itself.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can evaluate how a chosen digital platform algorithmically curates what I see, with evidence from my own observation or documentation.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band E.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I describe what I see on the platform but do not distinguish the curation from the content.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I claim curation is happening but rely on general assumptions rather than evidence from my own observation or documentation.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can evaluate how a chosen digital platform algorithmically curates what I see, with evidence from my own observation or documentation.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I evaluate curation with specific evidence (observations across time, comparisons between accounts, documented experiments) and identify patterns the algorithm seems to favour and suppress.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can explain at least two psychological or economic incentives driving a digital platform's design decisions.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band E.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I explain platform decisions as if they were neutral user-service choices, naming no incentives.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name one incentive (usually advertising revenue) but miss interacting psychological or economic incentives.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can explain at least two psychological or economic incentives driving a digital platform's design decisions.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I explain multiple incentives and show how they reinforce each other (for example, engagement metrics driving ad revenue while exploiting intermittent reinforcement).

criteria.criterion_statement: I can justify one specific change I will make to my use of a digital platform, showing why the change addresses the mechanism rather than the symptom.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band E.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I propose using the platform less without naming the mechanism I am targeting.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I propose a change that addresses a symptom (time on app) rather than a mechanism (the feature driving the time).
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can justify one specific change I will make to my use of a digital platform, showing why the change addresses the mechanism rather than the symptom.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I propose a change that directly targets a named mechanism, and I state how I will tell whether the change is actually disrupting the mechanism or just shifting the symptom elsewhere.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can evaluate the cumulative effect of persuasive design across a sustained period on my attention, relationships, and identity, drawing on my own evidence.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band F.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I evaluate effects in general terms without evidence from a sustained period of my own use.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I evaluate effects on one domain (usually attention) but miss effects on relationships or identity.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can evaluate the cumulative effect of persuasive design across a sustained period on my attention, relationships, and identity, drawing on my own evidence.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I evaluate cumulative effects across attention, relationships, and identity with evidence collected over a sustained period, including cases where the effect on one domain offset or amplified the effect on another.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can compare my analysis against at least two recognised pieces of research on digital wellbeing, including at least one that contests a claim I find sympathetic.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band F.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I cite research that agrees with me and ignore research that contests me.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I cite research on both sides but engage only with the side I already agree with.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can compare my analysis against at least two recognised pieces of research on digital wellbeing, including at least one that contests a claim I find sympathetic.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I compare my analysis against multiple pieces of research including contesting research, and I engage seriously with where the contesting work might be correct and my intuition wrong.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can articulate a reasoned personal position specifying where I will and will not allow these systems to operate on me, acknowledging what the evidence supports strongly, weakly, and where it remains uncertain.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band F.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I state a position without grading the evidence or specifying where the position applies.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I state a position with some graded evidence but my account treats all the evidence as roughly the same strength.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can articulate a reasoned personal position specifying where I will and will not allow these systems to operate on me, acknowledging what the evidence supports strongly, weakly, and where it remains uncertain.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I articulate a position with specific boundaries on where I will and will not allow these systems to operate, and I grade the underlying evidence at each point of the position.

criteria.criterion_statement: The student recognises, in context, that some of their own group memberships were unchosen and that in-group preference operates automatically — shown by the student naming how an unchosen membership or automatic in-group preference is shaping their own experience, reaction, or interpretation in a specific situation they are inside of, not only as an abstract claim.
observation_indicators: The student names an unchosen group membership they hold (family of origin, nationality, first language, how others read their body or appearance) AS it is shaping a specific situation they are inside of — spontaneously or when a situation makes it relevant — not only as an abstract claim that 'in-group preference exists'.

The student catches themselves, in a classroom discussion, group task, or social exchange, in a moment of automatic in-group preference (favouring a peer from their own group, dismissing an out-group perspective) and says so, without adult prompting to do so.

When interpreting a text, event, or peer's account, the student notes how an unchosen membership of theirs is shaping what they are noticing or missing — using concrete reference to the situation, not reciting the claim in the abstract.

In a cross-cultural or cross-group exchange, the student pauses and attributes their own reaction (confusion, irritation, quick agreement) to an unchosen membership or in-group preference, before concluding whose view is correct.
confusable_behaviours: Fluently stating the Band D Know claims ("unchosen group memberships shape experience", "in-group preference operates automatically") in the abstract when asked. Distinction: the disposition is visible only when the student applies the claim to a specific situation they are inside of, with enough concrete reference to show they are tracking the mechanism, not reciting it.

Pointing at other people's unchosen memberships or in-group preference ("that's why they voted that way", "that's why group X dismisses group Y") without ever examining the student's own. Distinction: Band D requires the student to locate the mechanism in their own perspective, not only in others.

Performative self-critique that foregrounds a single marked identity ("as a [nationality/gender/etc.] I…") in the way an adult audience expects, with no specific situation and no change in behaviour. Distinction: authentic recognition refers to a real moment of reaction and adjusts the student's response in it.
absence_indicators: Across a term, the student treats their own reactions, judgements, and interpretations as neutral observation — never locating any of their own views in an unchosen group membership or in-group preference, even when the situation plainly involves one.

When an out-group perspective is raised, the student dismisses it on merit without any self-check, showing no evidence that automatic in-group preference is recognisable to them as a mechanism operating in themselves.

When asked directly "how might your own background be shaping what you're noticing here?" the student either denies it applies or produces an off-the-shelf label without any situation-specific reference.
conversation_prompts: Think about a moment this month when you felt strongly that a peer, group, or source was right or wrong. Can you point at anything you didn't choose about your own background that might have been shaping that reaction?
Have you ever caught yourself preferring your own group without deciding to — not as a big claim, but in a specific moment? Tell me what you noticed.
When you read or watched [recent shared text/event], were there parts where something about your own background made some things obvious to you and other things easy to miss?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student voluntarily identifies, with specificity, how their own cultural lens — now analysable through the named Band E concepts (cultural lens, ethnocentrism, social identity theory, intersectionality) — is limiting or distorting their interpretation of a text, event, or interaction, in contexts where this is not explicitly required of them.
observation_indicators: In a discussion, essay, or reflection where lens analysis is not required, the student voluntarily identifies a specific way their cultural lens, ethnocentric assumption, in-group identification, or intersectional position is shaping what they are reading into the material — with enough specificity that the named concept is doing real work, not acting as label.

The student names a limit of their own interpretation before defending it — on more than one occasion across the term, and across more than one subject or context — using the Band E vocabulary correctly but not ornamentally.

In a cross-cultural or contested exchange, the student self-initiates a revision of their position after recognising that their reading was narrowed by their own lens, rather than waiting for the opposing view to push them.

In Reflection 360 or a comparable conversation, the student can trace a recent change in their own view to a specific lens-awareness move they made, with a concrete example rather than a generalised account.
confusable_behaviours: Deploying the Band E vocabulary (cultural lens, ethnocentrism, social identity theory, intersectionality) fluently when prompted to apply it, but never self-initiating the move in unprompted contexts. Distinction: Band E is dispositional — it requires the move to appear when nobody cued it.

Using the named concepts as blanket disclaimers ("of course my intersectional position shapes this") without any specific content or change in interpretation. Distinction: authentic lens awareness narrows the claim or changes the reading; it does not preface an unchanged position.

Analysing lens effects only in other people's views (a text author, a political speaker, an opposing peer) without turning the same analysis on the student's own position. Distinction: Band E requires the student to be inside the analysis, not only performing it on others.
absence_indicators: Across the term, the student uses the Band E concepts only when asked to, only in assessed tasks, and only on other people's views — never self-initiating lens analysis on their own position in unprompted contexts.

When faced with a text or event at the edge of the student's experience, the student defends their first interpretation on content grounds alone, with no evidence the named Band E concepts are available to them as working tools for self-analysis.
The student treats "everyone has a lens" as a conversation-ender rather than a starting point — used to deflect specificity rather than to do any specific self-examination.
conversation_prompts: Think of a recent opinion of yours that changed or sharpened. Can you say what about your own lens was limiting the earlier version — with specifics, not in general?

Where in the last month did you catch your own ethnocentrism, in-group identification, or a missing intersection in your reading of a situation, without anyone pointing it out to you? Tell me the situation.
When you use words like 'cultural lens' or 'intersectionality', are they doing real work in your thinking right now, or are they labels? Give me an example where they were doing real work.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can apply an integrative understanding of the stress-emotion-attention-habit system (drawing on mechanisms from Bands A–E) to a real decision or situation in my own life or community, choosing an action that addresses the system rather than a single symptom and justifying the action by specific mechanisms.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates applying integrative wellbeing-science understanding to a real decision or situation at Band F.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I describe a decision or situation but do not ground it in specific mechanisms from the stress-emotion-attention-habit system; or I cite mechanisms but do not link them to an observable action I took.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I ground an action in one named mechanism but do not integrate across the system or show how the action addresses more than a single symptom.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can apply an integrative understanding of the stress-emotion-attention-habit system to a real decision or situation, using specific mechanisms from Bands A–E to justify the action I chose and how it addresses the system rather than a single symptom.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I apply integrative understanding to a real decision, document the action and its effect across the system, and revise the action on the basis of what the system-level response revealed about the mechanisms at play.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can name my feelings using the specific emotion vocabulary — happy, sad, angry, scared, proud, ashamed, excited, surprised — when shown a scenario or picture prompt.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band A.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I attempt to name a feeling but use a general word only ('good', 'bad', 'fine') without selecting any of the specific emotion names from the Band A vocabulary set.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name at least one specific emotion from the Band A set correctly but apply the same label to two clearly different feelings shown in the same scenario.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can name my feelings using the specific emotion vocabulary — happy, sad, angry, scared, proud, ashamed, excited, surprised — when shown a scenario or picture prompt.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I name the distinct feelings of each character shown in the scenario and note that two characters in the same situation can feel differently — for example, one proud, one embarrassed.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe at least one specific way I want to be treated by my friends — such as being listened to, included, or spoken to kindly.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band A.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I say I want 'nice friends' or 'good friends' without naming any specific relational behaviour — what I want from a friend is not described in observable terms.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name a specific treatment preference such as 'I want friends to listen to me' but do not connect it to a situation or to why this matters to me.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can describe at least one specific way I want to be treated by my friends — such as being listened to, included, or spoken to kindly.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I describe how I want to be treated and also notice that what I need from a friend can shift depending on how I am feeling — for example, needing more space when upset.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe what a caring friendship looks like, identifying its observable and repeatable qualities — such as listening, kindness, fairness, and showing up when someone is upset.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band B.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I say a caring friendship means 'being nice' or 'not fighting' without identifying any specific observable and repeatable quality — such as listening, kindness, or fairness.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I identify one or two observable caring friendship qualities but describe them as one-time acts rather than repeating patterns — for example, 'a good friend helps you once when you're sad'.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can describe what a caring friendship looks like, identifying its observable and repeatable qualities — such as listening, kindness, fairness, and showing up when someone is upset.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I describe caring friendship qualities and contrast them with at least one specific uncaring quality — such as consistently making the other person feel ashamed — naming why the pattern, not the single incident, is what matters.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can explain how my feelings in a friendship change over time depending on whether the friendship is going well or not, connecting the emotional shift to specific friendship behaviours rather than single events.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band B.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I state that friendships feel different at different times but do not explain what causes the shift — I describe the emotional state without linking it to friendship behaviour.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I link my emotional change to a specific incident (a fight, a kind act) rather than to a pattern of how the friendship behaves across multiple situations over time.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can explain how my feelings in a friendship change over time depending on whether the friendship is going well or not, connecting the emotional shift to specific friendship behaviours rather than single events.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I explain feeling change over time and also note that the same friendship can move between going-well and not-going-well phases, making the overall pattern more informative than any single phase.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe how oestrogen and testosterone released during puberty increase the intensity of emotional reactions, and explain that this intensity is a normal developmental phase rather than a permanent trait.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band C.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I say emotions feel stronger during puberty without naming oestrogen and testosterone as the hormones involved, or without explaining that the intensity is a developmental phase rather than a permanent state.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I name oestrogen and testosterone correctly and link them to stronger emotional reactions, but describe the hormonal effect as permanent rather than as a developmental phase — for example, 'I will always feel this way'.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can describe how oestrogen and testosterone released during puberty increase the intensity of emotional reactions, and explain that this intensity is a normal developmental phase rather than a permanent trait.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I explain the hormone-emotion connection and also note that puberty's timing and intensity vary between individuals, which means the emotional experience of puberty differs across peer groups and relationships.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can identify patterns in a described relationship that distinguish caring from uncaring — where a pattern is a repeating tendency across multiple situations, not a single event.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band C.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I label a relationship as caring or uncaring based on a single incident in the scenario without identifying any repeated tendency across multiple situations.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I identify a repeated tendency — such as 'the friend is often dismissive' — but describe it in vague terms rather than naming the specific repeating behaviour (dismissal, control, deliberate disregard for feelings).
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can identify patterns in a described relationship that distinguish caring from uncaring — where a pattern is a repeating tendency across multiple situations, not a single event.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I identify the relational pattern and explain why it carries more weight than any single incident — including why one very caring act does not cancel a persistent uncaring pattern.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can explain the biological basis of an emotional experience using the PFC/amygdala maturation model and, for attraction, rejection, or loss, the corresponding neuroscientific account.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I mention the brain in relation to emotions but cannot name both the PFC and amygdala, or I reverse their roles — describing the amygdala as the part that 'thinks' and the PFC as the part that reacts fast.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I correctly name the PFC and amygdala and their maturational timelines but cannot apply this model to a specific emotional experience — I describe the system without connecting it to attraction, rejection, or loss.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can explain the PFC/amygdala maturation model and apply at least one of: elevated dopamine/norepinephrine for attraction, pain-overlap neural pathways for rejection, or non-linear grief for loss.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I explain the biological model accurately and flag where the evidence is provisional — noting that 'rejection activates pain pathways' is an interpretive inference, not a certainty equivalent to the PFC maturation timeline.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can identify healthy and unhealthy relational patterns in a described relationship using the Band D criteria: reciprocity, honest communication, boundary respect vs. control, emotional manipulation, and deliberate dependency-creation.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I label the described relationship as 'healthy' or 'unhealthy' overall but do not identify the specific patterns — such as emotional manipulation or deliberate inconsistency — that support the judgement.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I identify at least one specific healthy or unhealthy pattern in the described relationship but miss another pattern clearly present — for example, I notice control but not the deliberate inconsistency designed to create dependency.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can identify healthy and unhealthy relational patterns in a described relationship using the Band D criteria: reciprocity, honest communication, boundary respect vs. control, emotional manipulation, and deliberate dependency-creation.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I identify the relational patterns and also explain why certain unhealthy patterns — deliberate inconsistency, intermittent validation — are harder to detect from inside the relationship than from an observer's position.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can apply at least one of dual-process models, the stress-response window, or habit formation to analyse a personal or fictional experience of attraction, rejection, or loss, and identify the epistemic status of the claims I make.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I name a course framework (dual-process, stress-response window, or habit formation) but do not show how it applies to the specific experience of attraction, rejection, or loss I am analysing.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I apply one course framework to the experience coherently but treat all my claims as equally certain — I do not distinguish between empirical, interpretive, and normative claims in my analysis.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can apply at least one of dual-process models, the stress-response window, or habit formation to an experience of attraction, rejection, or loss, and correctly identify at least one claim as empirical, interpretive, or normative.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I apply one course framework coherently to the experience and consistently distinguish empirical, interpretive, and normative claims throughout — noting which claims I hold most tentatively and why.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can evaluate how biological, psychological, and social factors interact to shape a relational pattern — explaining how the combination produces outcomes that no single factor alone accounts for.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band E.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I list biological, psychological, and social factors as separate influences on the relational pattern without showing how they interact — the three factor types appear as parallel causes rather than as an interacting system.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I show how two of the three factor types interact in the relational pattern but treat the third as background context rather than demonstrating how its absence from my account changes the explanation.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can evaluate how biological, psychological, and social factors interact to shape a relational pattern — explaining how the combination produces outcomes that no single factor alone accounts for.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I evaluate the three-factor interaction and identify which factor type is most explanatory for this specific relational pattern while explaining what the de-emphasised factors would add if foregrounded.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can compare the perspectives of the people involved in a relational pattern, explaining how the same behaviours or events may be experienced differently from each person's position.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band E.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I describe the relational pattern from one person's perspective and acknowledge that the other person sees it differently, but I do not explain what causes the difference in their experiences.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I describe both perspectives in the relational pattern but treat them as equally valid without identifying the relational history, role, or emotional investment that explains why their views diverge.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can compare the perspectives of the people involved in a relational pattern, explaining what relational history, role, or emotional investment causes the same events to be experienced differently.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I compare perspectives and also identify a dimension of the dynamic where neither person's account is fully accurate — for example, both systematically under- or overestimating how the other is affected.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can explain how power or context affects the relational dynamic I am analysing, identifying at least two of the six observable power markers from the Band E framework.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band E.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I note that power is relevant to the relational dynamic without applying any of the six observable power markers — I say 'there is a power imbalance' without identifying what specific behaviour reveals it.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I identify one observable power marker in the dynamic (such as who apologises more often regardless of fault) but do not explain how this marker shapes the relational pattern over time.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can explain how power or context affects the relational dynamic I am analysing, identifying at least two of the six power markers — such as who sets the emotional tone, who apologises regardless of fault, or who bears the greater cost.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I identify power markers and explain how cultural or contextual norms make those markers feel normal or invisible to the people inside the relationship — accounting for why power is often hardest to see from within.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can critically evaluate a complex relational dynamic using the named four-framework set: attachment theory, dual-process models of emotion and cognition, social learning theory, and power/context analysis.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band F.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I name frameworks from the four-framework set but apply them illustratively — using vocabulary (attachment style, dual-process) without showing how the framework changes my reading of the relational dynamic.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I apply one framework from the named set (attachment theory, dual-process models, social learning theory, or power/context analysis) coherently to the dynamic but do not use a second to cross-examine or extend the analysis.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I use at least two frameworks from the named set (attachment theory, dual-process models, social learning theory, power/context analysis) to analyse the dynamic, with each framework illuminating a dimension the other misses.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I use three or more frameworks and, for at least one (e.g. attachment theory), distinguish empirical core from contested interpretations and popularised distortions — explaining what each framework illuminates and obscures in this case.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can explain how biological, psychological, social, and cultural factors intersect in a complex relational dynamic — including how cultural scripts and historical power norms shape what feels natural in the relationship.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band F.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I name biological, psychological, social, and cultural factors as relevant to the dynamic but treat each layer separately — I do not show where the layers interact or amplify each other's effects.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: I show how two or three factor layers interact in the dynamic but treat the cultural layer — cultural scripts for what is normal or appropriate in relationships — as background context rather than as an active analytical category.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can explain how all four factor layers (biological, psychological, social, cultural) intersect in the dynamic — including how cultural scripts shape what feels natural or expected within the relationship.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: I explain the four-factor intersection and evaluate which layers are most explanatory for this specific relational dynamic while acknowledging what the less emphasised layers would reveal if foregrounded.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can construct a justified position on how a relational dynamic might be navigated or improved, demonstrating evidentiary fit, acknowledgement of alternatives, framework transparency, and acknowledgement of limits.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band F.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: I offer a recommendation on how the relational dynamic might be navigated but it is not tied to my analysis — evidentiary fit is absent and the position is asserted rather than derived from what I found.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: My position has evidentiary fit — it is grounded in my analysis — but I do not acknowledge that a different framework choice or reading could produce a different conclusion (alternatives and framework transparency are missing).
competency_level_descriptors.competent: My position is tied to evidence (evidentiary fit), acknowledges at least one alternative reading, names the framework I am primarily relying on and why I chose it, and identifies one thing my framework cannot adequately account for.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: My position demonstrates all four dimensions — evidentiary fit, two or more alternatives considered, explicit framework choice with reasoning, and named limits — integrated as a coherent argument rather than as a sequential checklist.

criteria.criterion_statement: The student, when experiencing a strong feeling (anger, fear, distress, or overwhelm), accepts a trusted adult's co-regulation support — following a guided breath, moving to a calm space with adult accompaniment, or receiving appropriate physical comfort where school and family norms permit — rather than pulling away, resisting, or remaining locked in the activated state.
observation_indicators: When a trusted adult sits near and breathes slowly, the student visibly reduces their own breathing rate or physical tension within two to three minutes — observable as relaxed shoulders, slower movement, or return of eye contact.
When offered a move to the calm space, the student goes (even reluctantly) rather than refusing to leave the activated situation — and returns to the class activity within the observation window.
When an adult offers a choice of regulation options ('do you want water or the quiet corner?'), the student selects one and engages with it, rather than rejecting both or escalating further.
Across multiple activation episodes in the observation window, the student's time from peak activation to resumed engagement is decreasing when adult co-regulation is offered.
confusable_behaviours: Compliance without regulation: the student goes to the calm space because they were told to but shows no reduction in physiological activation (still crying, hitting objects, or showing extreme facial tension throughout). Distinction: the criterion requires visible de-escalation, not only location change.

Social display without genuine activation: the student performs emotional distress for adult attention but does not meet the threshold of genuine activation. Distinguish by checking whether triggers are consistent with the student's known activation profile across contexts and informants.
absence_indicators: Across multiple activation episodes, the student consistently resists or rejects adult co-regulation offers — pushing the adult away, running, or escalating further when the trusted adult approaches.
The student's activation episodes consistently require adult-led management with no observable reduction in activation even when a trusted adult is fully present and engaged.
conversation_prompts: When a feeling is really big, who helps you feel better? What do they do?
Show me what you do when you feel scared or really angry.
What does it feel like in your body when the big feeling starts to go away?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student, during or shortly after experiencing emotional activation, attempts at least one named regulation strategy — such as deliberate breathing, squeezing a stress object, moving to a designated calm area, or requesting water — with a trusted adult nearby or having recently offered support.
observation_indicators: The student reaches for a specific classroom regulation object (a squeeze ball, a sensory item, a calm-down kit) during an episode of emotional activation — the reach is intentional and directed, not random exploratory play.
The student takes three or more slow, deliberate breaths when a feeling is strong — observable as controlled chest or belly movement rather than continued distress crying or shouting.
The student uses a named strategy from the class's shared regulation vocabulary (e.g. 'I'm going to do my star breathing') during or after an activation episode, with or without full effectiveness.

Across the observation window, strategy attempts are becoming more consistent — not always fully effective, but the attempt occurs during the activation episode rather than only after adult direction.
confusable_behaviours: Distraction without strategy: the student grabs any available object or seeks a different activity without connection to the class's named regulation strategy set. Distinction: the criterion requires the attempt to be a recognisable regulation strategy, not any object-seeking or avoidance behaviour.

Naming a strategy without attempting it: the student says 'I need to do my breathing' but does not follow through with any observable attempt. Distinction: the criterion requires a physical attempt, however brief or incomplete.
absence_indicators: Across the observation window, no strategy attempt is observable during activation episodes — the student discharges the feeling through action (hitting, running, screaming) without any named strategy use before or during the episode.
The student's only regulation behaviour is seeking adult presence; no attempt at a named strategy is observable even when the trusted adult prompts one.
conversation_prompts: When something makes you feel really upset, what do you try to do?
Tell me about a time when a big feeling got smaller. What happened?
What's your favourite way to calm down?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student, when emotionally activated during class, uses a named regulation strategy (counting slowly, using a movement card, going to the quiet corner, drawing feelings) when a trusted adult prompts them to do so — and shows visible de-escalation from the strategy use within the same observation session.
observation_indicators: When an adult says 'can you try your counting?' or 'would the quiet corner help right now?', the student engages with the suggested strategy rather than refusing or escalating.
After using the adult-prompted strategy, the student's physiological activation visibly reduces — observable as return to seated position, decreased volume, or willingness to re-engage with a task.
Across multiple observed activation episodes with adult prompting, the student's latency to engaging the strategy (time from prompt to strategy use) is decreasing across the observation window.

The student uses the prompted strategy for a duration that reflects genuine engagement — three breath cycles rather than one token breath; two minutes at the quiet corner rather than an immediate return with no visible change.
confusable_behaviours: Inert compliance: the student goes to the quiet corner when prompted but shows no activation of any regulation behaviour there — not breathing deliberately, not using any regulation tool — and returns in the same activated state. Distinction: the criterion requires visible de-escalation, not only location compliance.

Calming through trigger removal: the student calms because the emotional trigger (the conflict, the task) is no longer present rather than because a strategy was used. Distinguish by checking whether the observable strategy behaviour is present and active during the de-escalation.
absence_indicators: When the adult prompts a regulation strategy during activation, the student consistently refuses, escalates in response to the prompt, or requires extensive negotiation before engaging.
The student only de-escalates when the adult is continuously present and actively managing the episode — no strategy use is observable even with explicit prompting.
conversation_prompts: When your teacher reminds you to try something when you're upset — what do you usually try?
How do you know when the calm-down strategy is working?
Is there a strategy you like better than others? Why does that one help?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student, on some occasions across the observation window, uses a named regulation strategy without an adult suggesting or prompting it — approaching the calm corner before the adult intervenes, taking a deliberate breath before reacting, or requesting a movement break before reaching peak activation.
observation_indicators: On at least two occasions across the observation window, the student moves to the calm corner or reaches for a regulation tool without the adult having spoken first — the adult witnesses the move, not prompted it.
The student says 'I need a minute' or similar before an adult has noticed any escalation — the student's self-initiated request precedes adult observation of the activation.
After an unprompted strategy use, the student can name what they did in conversation: 'I went and breathed for a bit' — demonstrating that the behaviour was intentional, not random.
Self-initiated strategy use occurs before peak activation on at least one occasion — the student is acting on an early-signal, not waiting until fully overwhelmed.
confusable_behaviours: Avoidance disguised as self-regulation: the student frequently leaves the classroom or requests breaks to escape difficult tasks or social situations rather than to regulate emotion. Distinction: the criterion requires the self-initiation to follow identifiable emotional activation (rising frustration, visible upset), not task-avoidance preconditions.

Classroom-routine cuing: the student uses a strategy because the class always does a regulation check-in at that scheduled time, not from genuine self-monitoring. Distinguish by noting whether self-initiated use occurs outside structured routine moments.
absence_indicators: Across the full observation window, every observed strategy use by the student follows an adult prompt — no self-initiated regulation behaviour is recorded on any occasion.
The student's self-regulation episodes are fully reactive: the student consistently reaches high activation before any strategy is engaged, with no observable early-signal monitoring.
conversation_prompts: Have you ever decided to do something to calm down before a grown-up told you to? What happened?
What does it feel like in your body just before a big feeling arrives?
What's one thing you can do by yourself when a feeling starts getting big?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student, in familiar emotional situations they have encountered before — peer conflict with a known classmate, frustration during collaborative work, disappointment about a result they care about — uses a named regulation strategy without an adult prompting it, and returns to functional engagement with the activity, person, or group.
observation_indicators: When conflict arises with a classmate the student knows well, the student steps back and uses a strategy (leaves briefly to breathe, writes it down, physically distances for a minute) then returns to negotiate — without the adult intervening to broker the de-escalation.
When frustrated by a task that has previously caused emotional activation in this student, their first observable response is a self-regulation move rather than complaint, shutdown, or aggression.

After an emotional episode in a familiar situation, the student re-engages with the original activity within a reasonable time — functionally present, not necessarily happy — without adult orchestration of the return.
Across the observation window, independent strategy use is stable across the student's different familiar emotional triggers — not confined to one specific situation type they have rehearsed.
confusable_behaviours: Physical distancing without strategy: the student walks away from a conflict but shows no regulation behaviour during separation — returns in the same activated state. Distinction: the criterion requires a visible strategy use during or as part of the distancing, not only distance.

Emotional masking: the student presents a calm exterior through suppression but is observable as still activated (rigid posture, flat affect, sustained avoidance of the situation) once the adult's attention moves elsewhere. Look for genuine re-engagement as evidence of de-escalation, not only surface calm.
absence_indicators: In familiar emotional situations, the student consistently requires adult prompting before any regulation strategy is used — independent strategy use is not yet established even in contexts the student has encountered many times.
Familiar emotional triggers consistently produce the same escalation pattern with no observable self-regulation move before or during the episode.
conversation_prompts: When you and a classmate have an argument, what do you usually do? Has that changed this year?
Walk me through what happened the last time something at school upset you — what did you do first?
What's the hardest feeling for you to manage on your own? What do you do when it arrives?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student, in a situation producing emotional activation, either: interrupts an automatic reaction before acting on it — pausing before snapping back, writing instead of shouting, turning away before retaliating — OR proactively seeks a trusted adult before the situation exceeds their capacity, approaching the adult while still manageable rather than only after full escalation.
observation_indicators: The student is observed pausing mid-automatic sequence — beginning to respond impulsively, then visibly stopping (mouth closes, hand drops, body turns away) before completing the reactive action — on at least one occasion across the observation window.

The student approaches a trusted adult to say 'I'm having a really hard time' or similar before the adult has noticed an incident — the student's approach precedes the escalation visible to the adult.
When asked why they came to the adult, the student can articulate an early signal: 'I could feel I was about to do something I'd regret' or 'I knew I was getting too angry to sort it out alone'.

Across the observation window, the student's proactive help-seeking is selective — they distinguish between situations they can manage independently and those where they recognise they need support before escalation.
confusable_behaviours: Situation-escape labelled as proactive help-seeking: the student approaches the adult to escape a difficult task or conflict rather than because they recognise a genuine escalation risk. Distinguish by whether the student can name the early emotional signal that triggered the approach — not just 'I didn't want to be there'.

Post-escalation apology: the student apologises for an automatic reaction after it has run to completion, which shows awareness but not the interruption the criterion requires. Distinction: the criterion requires the interruption or approach to occur before the reactive action completes.
absence_indicators: Across the observation window, automatic reactions consistently run to completion before any interruption is observable — the student always acts first and reflects after.
The student never approaches a trusted adult before an incident has already occurred — help-seeking is always post-escalation or adult-initiated.
conversation_prompts: Tell me about a time when you almost did something but stopped yourself. What happened inside you in that moment?
How do you know when you're about to go over the edge? What does it feel like?
Have you ever gone to a teacher before something went wrong? What made you do that?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student, across multiple different emotional situations over the observation window — not only familiar or previously practised contexts — applies taught regulation strategies in practice: using them behaviourally, not only naming them, and returning to functional engagement after use.
observation_indicators: Regulation strategy use is observable across at least three distinct situation types across the observation window (e.g. peer conflict, academic pressure, public correction, unexpected change) — the student is not confining strategy use to one familiar context type.

The student uses regulation strategies in situations that have not been specifically role-played or coached — applying their repertoire to novel emotional triggers without prior adult guidance for that specific situation.

Strategy use is behavioural and observable: the teacher sees the student doing the strategy (leaving the room briefly, breathing deliberately, writing in a journal) rather than only reporting having strategies when asked.

After strategy use across multiple situations, the student returns to the activity with observable function — participating in discussion, completing work, re-engaging with a peer — not requiring prolonged adult transition support.
confusable_behaviours: Strategy rhetoric without practice: the student articulates their regulation strategies fluently in class discussion but shows no observable use during actual activation episodes. Distinction: this criterion requires visible behavioural use in the moment, not verbal account of strategies.

Single-context application: the student uses strategies well in one type of familiar situation but consistently shows no strategy use in other contexts. Distinction: the criterion requires multi-situation evidence; single-context application is Band C, not Band D.
absence_indicators: Regulation strategy use is absent or consistently adult-prompted in situations the student has not been specifically coached through.

Across the observation window, the student's emotional activation episodes produce the same reactive response regardless of strategy availability — strategies are described but not accessed in the moment across varied contexts.
conversation_prompts: Think of three times this term when you had a strong feeling. What did you do in each one — was it the same approach each time?
Is there a situation where it's harder to use your strategies? What makes that one different?
When you're in a situation you haven't been in before, how do you know what to do?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student recognises, in the moment, when they are approaching or exceeding their stress-response window — noticing narrowing attention, rising irritability, or decreasing capacity to engage — and seeks help from a trusted adult or deploys a regulation strategy before capacity is lost.
observation_indicators: The student says 'I think I'm getting close to my limit' or 'I can feel I'm about to lose it' and requests a regulation support — a break, a conversation, a change of task — before the adult has independently identified the escalation.

When the student's window-proximity is visible to an observer (increased motor activity, withdrawal, shortened responses), the student initiates a regulation move independently rather than waiting for an adult prompt.

The student uses window-of-tolerance vocabulary ('I'm outside my window', 'I need to get back into my window') as a practical self-monitoring report in real time — not only in reflection after an episode has resolved.
After a proactive help-seek at the window edge, the student re-enters the activity — demonstrating that the action was a self-management move, not an escape from the situation.
confusable_behaviours: Social complaint without self-monitoring: the student frequently says 'this is too much' or 'I can't do this' as social communication (frustration venting, bids for sympathy) without any associated regulation action or proactive help-seek. Distinction: window recognition is evidenced by a proactive action following the verbal report, not by verbal expression alone.

Learned vocabulary without real-time monitoring: the student uses window-of-tolerance language fluently in reflection and discussion but does not apply it as a monitoring tool during actual activation episodes. Distinction: the indicator requires in-the-moment application, not only post-hoc vocabulary use in safe reflection contexts.
absence_indicators: Across the observation window, the student consistently reaches full overwhelm before any self-monitoring move — there is no record of window-proximity recognition followed by a proactive action.
Help-seeking consistently follows escalation rather than preceding it — the student seeks adult support only after losing capacity, not before.
conversation_prompts: What does it feel like in your body when you're getting close to the edge? How do you know before it happens?
Have you ever stopped something before it got out of hand because you recognised the signal? What did that feel like?
When you feel yourself losing your ability to think clearly, what do you do?

criteria.criterion_statement: Following a relational rupture in which the student has contributed to a conflict or hurt, the student initiates a repair move — acknowledging their part in the incident and proposing a forward step — without waiting for the teacher or the other person to broker the conversation.
observation_indicators: After a conflict, the student approaches the other party and says something that acknowledges their contribution: 'I shouldn't have said that', 'I walked off and that made it worse', 'I was the one who started it' — naming their own part specifically.

The student's repair approach is not prompted by teacher instruction — the teacher observes the student moving toward the other party of their own initiative, not after being told 'you should go and sort this out'.
The repair move includes a forward step — 'do you want to sit together at lunch?' or 'can we try again?' — not only an acknowledgement. The student proposes resumption, not only expresses regret.
The student attempts repair on at least one occasion across the observation window — not every conflict needs to produce a repair attempt, but at least one student-initiated repair event is recorded.
confusable_behaviours: Teacher-brokered apology: the student apologises only after the teacher explicitly says 'go and say sorry' or arranges the repair conversation. Distinction: the criterion requires the student to initiate the repair move independently, not in response to explicit adult direction.

Defensive acknowledgement: the student acknowledges the incident in a way that deflects responsibility — 'I'm sorry if you felt upset' or 'I was upset too'. Distinction: genuine repair initiation includes acknowledgement of the student's own contribution; symmetrising or conditional language ('I'm sorry you felt...') performs repair while diluting accountability.
absence_indicators: Across the observation window, no student-initiated repair move is recorded — all post-conflict resolutions are teacher-brokered or left unresolved.
The student's post-conflict behaviour is consistently avoidance (ignoring the other party) or externalisation (framing the other person as solely responsible) without any repair approach.
conversation_prompts: Think about a conflict you had with someone recently. Who went to sort it out first? What did you say?
What makes it hard to be the one to go first after an argument?
Has there been a time when you went to sort something out and it went well? What did you do?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student applies regulation strategies in situations that are not familiar or practised — new social groups, unexpected failure, relational conflicts outside their usual friendships, high-pressure performance contexts — without adult prompting and without requiring a prior adult coaching session for that specific situation.
observation_indicators: When placed in an unfamiliar social situation (new group project, class transition, out-of-school context triangulated by another informant), the student uses a regulation strategy without first seeking adult guidance on what to do.

When an unexpected event produces emotional activation in a context the student has not been coached through, the student's first response is a self-regulatory move — brief deliberate withdrawal, controlled breathing, writing — not externalisation or adult-seeking.

After regulation strategy use in an unfamiliar context, the student can describe what they did and why it was the right move for that situation — demonstrating transfer of judgment, not only procedural repetition of a practised strategy.

Cross-context application is documented across at least two informant sources across the observation window — the student's self-regulation is not confined to contexts where their primary teacher is present.
confusable_behaviours: Familiar-context strategy use only: the student uses strategies reliably in coached, familiar situations but shows no observable strategy use in genuinely novel contexts. Distinction: the criterion requires evidence from situations that have not been specifically practised — not only the student's established regulation situations.

Social masking in unfamiliar contexts: the student appears calm in unfamiliar settings but shows physiological activation signals (hypervigilance, clenching, post-context dysregulation reported by another informant) that suggest suppression rather than regulation. Cross-informant triangulation is required before assigning this component.
absence_indicators: Strategy use is consistently absent in contexts outside the student's regular classroom or established peer group — transfer to novel contexts has not yet appeared.

The student requires an adult conversation about the specific unfamiliar situation before any strategy use is observable — they cannot access their regulation repertoire independently in novel contexts.
conversation_prompts: Think of a time when you were in a situation you hadn't been in before and something went wrong emotionally. What did you do?
Is there a context where it's hard to use the things that usually help you? What's different about that one?
When no one who knows you well is around, how do you manage when something difficult happens?

criteria.criterion_statement: Following a significant emotional setback — academic failure with ongoing consequences, peer rejection, a public embarrassment, or a loss — the student recovers to functional engagement with commitments (class, peers, after-school activities) within a timeframe proportionate to the severity of the setback, using self-directed strategies rather than sustained withdrawal or adult-orchestrated return.
observation_indicators: After a significant setback, the student's re-engagement is student-initiated: they return to class participation, reach out to peers, or communicate readiness to resume without waiting for a teacher or pastoral lead to initiate the return.

The student's recovery trajectory is observable: the teacher can document the student moving from acute reaction (withdrawal, distress) through a visible self-directed phase (using a strategy, talking to a trusted peer) to functional re-engagement — the recovery is not simply time passing.
The student can describe in conversation what they did to manage the setback — naming a specific action or decision they took, not only reporting that time helped or that they felt better eventually.

After recovery, the student re-engages with the domain of the setback (returns to the subject, attempts the relationship again, re-submits work) rather than avoiding the domain — demonstrating that recovery includes re-engagement, not only emotional stabilisation.
confusable_behaviours: Time-as-treatment: the student eventually resumes normal functioning because the emotional intensity faded with time, not because of any observable self-directed recovery strategy. Distinction: the criterion requires evidence of a self-directed process during recovery, not only eventual return to baseline.

Adult-orchestrated return the student then sustains: a pastoral lead or parent makes the first contact that re-engages the student, after which the student sustains engagement independently. Distinguish by noting who initiated the first contact — the criterion requires student initiation of the return.
absence_indicators: After significant setbacks, the student's withdrawal extends well beyond a proportionate timeframe with no observable self-directed recovery behaviour before adult intervention.
The student's return to functioning consistently depends on adult-orchestrated support — the recovery does not begin without sustained adult management.
conversation_prompts: Tell me about a time this year when something big went wrong — how did you get through it? What did you do for yourself?
After a setback, how do you know when you're ready to re-engage? What does that feel like?
Is there something that happened that you found really hard to come back from? What helped, in the end?

criteria.criterion_statement: When a regulation strategy the student usually relies on is not reducing their emotional activation in a particular episode — the feeling is not decreasing, the body is not calming, engagement is not returning — the student notices this, stops using the ineffective strategy, and shifts to a different one rather than persisting with the same approach or abandoning self-regulation effort entirely.
observation_indicators: The teacher observes the student try a strategy (deep breathing, journalling, using the calm corner), show no visible de-escalation after sustained use, then shift to a different strategy — not prompted by adult observation or suggestion.

The student names the shift in conversation: 'I tried my breathing but it wasn't working so I went for a walk instead' — demonstrating awareness that the first strategy was insufficient and the switch was intentional.
After the adapted strategy, the student's activation reduces and they return to engagement — the adaptation produced the outcome that the first strategy alone did not.

Across the observation window, the student's strategy repertoire in practice is actively varied rather than fixed — they are selecting among strategies based on the situation, not always defaulting to the same approach.
confusable_behaviours: Abandoning self-regulation altogether: the student tries a strategy, finds it insufficient, then stops trying to regulate — escalating further or withdrawing rather than shifting to an alternative. Distinction: the criterion requires a deliberate shift to a different strategy, not simply stopping the first one.

Intensifying the same strategy: the student takes one breath, it doesn't work, so they take twenty more — persisting with the same approach with greater intensity rather than reconsidering strategy type. Distinction: the criterion requires a change in strategy type, not escalation of the same approach.
absence_indicators: When the student's usual strategy is not working, they consistently default to one response: persisting with the same strategy regardless of effect, or abandoning self-regulation effort entirely.

No observation of deliberate strategy-switching is recorded across the observation window — the student's regulation repertoire, in practice, is fixed to one approach regardless of its effectiveness in the episode.
conversation_prompts: Has there been a time when what usually helps you just wasn't working? What did you do then?
How do you know when a strategy isn't doing what you need it to do?
What's your backup plan when your main strategy doesn't help?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student manages their own emotional activation in high-stakes or accumulating-stress situations — national examinations, competitive performances, serious personal crises, or prolonged relational difficulties — without requiring adult scaffolding during the event itself: using self-directed strategies, sustaining focus across the duration, and returning to functional engagement after.
observation_indicators: During or immediately before a high-stakes event (major examination, formal performance, an important difficult conversation), the student uses a self-directed regulation strategy observable to a present teacher or confirmed through student report triangulated by cross-context evidence.

Across a sustained high-stress period (exam season, a difficult period in a significant relationship, an ongoing family situation), the student maintains participation in commitments — class attendance, work submission, peer relationships — without adult orchestration of their continued engagement.

In conversation after a high-stakes event, the student can describe what regulation strategies they used during the event — evidence that strategies were deployed in real time, not only accessed in retrospect.

The teacher or trusted adult has NOT been orchestrating the student's functioning during this period — the student's continued engagement is self-maintained, with adult presence being available but not managing.
confusable_behaviours: Dissociation or emotional numbing: the student appears unaffected by high-stakes situations not because they are regulating effectively but because they have learned to suppress or disconnect from emotional experience. Distinguish through longitudinal observation of emotional responsiveness across contexts: a student who regulates well also shows proportionate emotional responses in lower-stakes situations. Dissociation tends to produce affect flatness that extends beyond the high-stakes event.

Invisible adult scaffolding: a parent, mentor, therapist, or pastoral lead is actively managing the student's functioning in the high-stakes domain without this being visible in the classroom. Cross-check with pastoral team before assigning Band F evidence on this component.
absence_indicators: High-stakes or prolonged-stress situations consistently produce loss of function that requires adult orchestration to restore — the student cannot sustain participation without ongoing adult management during the event.
The student avoids high-stakes contexts rather than managing within them — declining opportunities, finding reasons to be absent, or withdrawing from commitments when stress accumulates.
conversation_prompts: Think about the most high-stakes moment of this year for you. What did you do for yourself during it?
When stress has been building for a long time and doesn't go away, what keeps you going?
Is there a time this year when you were in a really hard situation and got through it without anyone managing it for you? What did that take?
